content: Bug fix: storage.get_memory() raises ValueError for missing memories, but manager.py search paths (lines 541, 571) and crossref lookup (line 899) expected it to return None. Fixed by wrapping calls in try/except ValueError to gracefully skip deleted/missing memories referenced by stale search indices.

require-task-close rule fix: The stop-gate rule at install/shared/rules/stop-gates/require-task-close.yaml had two bugs. (1) The Jinja template used {{ task_ref }} which is never set in the rule engine's eval context — only in commit_policy.py's context. Fixed to use {{ claimed_task_id[:8] if claimed_task_id else '(unknown)' }} which is available via _build_eval_context() flattening workflow variables. (2) The when condition didn't guard against empty claimed_task_id, causing the rule to fire with blank task IDs. Added `and variables.get('claimed_task_id')` guard.

LLM generate_text interface now supports max_tokens parameter across all providers (base.py, claude.py, gemini.py, litellm.py, codex.py). SDK path in claude.py post-truncates since ClaudeAgentOptions doesn't support max_tokens. LiteLLM paths pass max_tokens directly to acompletion. Default is 8000 when not specified. Session title synthesis uses model='haiku' with max_tokens=30 to prevent unbounded output.

Sentence collision bug root cause: In websocket/chat.py, the TextChunk handler checked `if accumulated_text and not accumulated_text.endswith(...)` to inject a \n\n separator after tool calls. But accumulated_text was already flushed to '' by the ToolCallEvent handler, making the condition always False. Fix: added a `has_sent_text` boolean flag (initialized False, set True when non-empty text is sent) that survives the flush. The separator check now uses `if has_sent_text` instead of checking accumulated_text.

ExitPlanMode approval gate pattern: In chat_session_permissions.py, ExitPlanMode now uses the same asyncio.Event blocking pattern as AskUserQuestion. It reads the plan file, broadcasts plan_pending_approval to the frontend, then blocks on _pending_plan_event.wait() with 600s timeout. The frontend shows the approval bar and opens the plan in the artifact panel. User decision routes through session_control.py → provide_plan_decision() which sets _pending_plan_decision and unblocks the event. Approve → set_chat_mode('accept_edits') + PermissionResultAllow. Reject → PermissionResultDeny with feedback so agent stays in plan mode.

Session title synthesis: Updated to use PromptLoader system (sessions/synthesize_title prompt), model='haiku' instead of default opus, max_tokens=30. Post-truncation safety net: first line only, strip quotes, cap at 100 chars. Manual rename validates title <= 200 chars (400 error). New prompt file at src/gobby/install/shared/prompts/sessions/synthesize_title.md.

LLM generate_text max_tokens parameter: Added max_tokens parameter to LLMProvider.generate_text() abstract interface and all 4 implementations (claude, gemini, litellm, codex). For Claude SDK path, max_tokens can't be passed to ClaudeAgentOptions, so post-truncation is used (result[:max_tokens*4]). For LiteLLM/OpenAI paths, it's passed directly to acompletion/create. Default remains 8000 when not specified.

Sentence collision fix in websocket/chat.py: The has_sent_text flag tracks whether any text was sent in the current turn, independent of accumulated_text flushes. The old check `if accumulated_text and not accumulated_text.endswith(...)` was always False because accumulated_text was already flushed to '' by ToolCallEvent handler before the separator check ran. The fix uses `has_sent_text` which survives the flush.

ExitPlanMode approval gate pattern: In chat_session_permissions.py, ExitPlanMode now blocks using asyncio.Event (same pattern as AskUserQuestion) until user approves or requests changes via provide_plan_decision(). The backend sends a 'plan_pending_approval' WS message to the frontend with plan_content read from _plan_file_path. On approve, it sets mode to accept_edits and broadcasts mode_changed with reason 'plan_approved'. On request_changes, it denies the tool with feedback so agent stays in plan mode. The frontend handles this in useChat.ts with a dedicated handler that sets planPendingApproval=true and triggers artifact creation via onPlanReadyRef callback.

Session #1748: Implemented Drawbridge UI tasks. (1) Removed WHEN/EFFECT code blocks from RulesTab.tsx - deleted RuleCodeBlock, codeTheme, syntax-highlighter imports, expand state/props. YAML button remains as sole detail viewer. (2) Font consistency across 4 CSS files: converted hardcoded px to calc(var(--font-size-base) * factor). Scale: titles=1.0 (16px), counts=1.25 (20px), names=0.875 (14px), descriptions/inputs=0.75 (12px), badges=0.625 (10px). Removed var(--font-mono) from skills-row-name, skills-detail-title, agent form inputs (monospace only for code/IDs). Commit: 72fdfe97.

Rules cards no longer show WHEN/EFFECT/MATCH code blocks on expand. The RuleCodeBlock component and react-syntax-highlighter dependency were removed from RulesTab.tsx. The YAML editor modal (via the YAML button) is the sole way to view/edit rule details. The expanded state, detail loading, and fetchRuleDetail-on-expand logic were all removed.

Web UI font consistency: admin pages (Agents, Skills, MCP Servers, Rules/Workflows) now use calc(var(--font-size-base) * factor) instead of hardcoded px values. The scale: 1.0 for page titles (16px), 1.25 for overview counts (20px), 0.875 for item names (14px), 0.75 for descriptions/inputs/buttons (12px), 0.625 for badges/labels (10px). Chat page uses Tailwind text-sm (14px) for content and text-xs (12px) for metadata. Skills row names and detail titles no longer use var(--font-mono) — monospace is reserved for code/IDs only (e.g., .mcp-tool-name, .mcp-detail-header h3).

AppErrorBoundary exists in web/src/App.tsx wrapping all tab content. It accepts activeTab and onReturnToChat props. It auto-resets error state when the user switches tabs (componentDidUpdate checks prevProps.activeTab). Shows error message, 'Try Again' button (resets error state), and 'Return to Chat' button. This prevents any tab crash from unmounting the entire React tree — the sidebar and shell remain intact.

Web UI code splitting pattern: All non-default page components in App.tsx are lazy-loaded with React.lazy() and wrapped in Suspense inside the AppErrorBoundary. Vendor deps are split via manualChunks in vite.config.ts: codemirror, xterm, syntax-highlighter, markdown, d3. MemoryGraph (2D) and KnowledgeGraph (3D) are both lazy-loaded in MemoryPage.tsx. The lazy import pattern is: const Page = lazy(() => import('./components/Page').then(m => ({ default: m.Page }))). ChatPage stays eagerly loaded as the default tab.

React error boundaries only catch synchronous render/lifecycle errors. Async errors from Three.js/WebGL (in requestAnimationFrame loops) escape them entirely. In React 18, unhandled errors cause the entire root tree to unmount and re-mount with default state. Fix pattern: (1) App-level ErrorBoundary wrapping tab content for synchronous crash recovery, (2) window.addEventListener('error'/'unhandledrejection') with keyword filtering for async WebGL/Three.js errors, (3) canvas.addEventListener('webglcontextlost') for GPU context loss. Use refs for callbacks in window listeners to avoid stale closures without re-registering listeners.

Three plan-mode bugs filed: #9026 (P1) plan mode exits without showing plan content to user, #9027 (P2) plans never written to artifact/preview window, #9028 (P1) ExitPlanMode doesn't require actual user approval — auto-succeeds with no user interaction gate. The entire plan-mode review flow is currently non-functional: agent writes file nobody sees, 'requests approval' that auto-succeeds, starts coding. Fix likely in hooks system — ExitPlanMode callback needs to render plan in UI and block on user confirmation.

React 18 error boundary limitation: Error boundaries only catch errors during rendering, lifecycle methods, and constructors. They do NOT catch errors in event handlers, async code (setTimeout, requestAnimationFrame, promises). Three.js render loops use requestAnimationFrame, so WebGL crashes bypass error boundaries entirely. When no error boundary catches a render error in React 18, the ENTIRE root tree is unmounted (different from React 17 which left corrupted tree mounted).

Memory tab crash root cause: When Neo4j is configured, viewMode auto-switches to 'knowledge' which lazy-loads KnowledgeGraph.tsx (Three.js/WebGL). With 955 entities + 2238 relationships, ForceGraph3D creates 955 SpriteText objects (each with canvas texture). Three.js crashes happen in requestAnimationFrame (async), which React error boundaries CANNOT catch. No global ErrorBoundary in App.tsx means React 18 unmounts the entire root tree, and activeTab resets to default 'chat'. Fix: add App-level ErrorBoundary, lower DEFAULT_KNOWLEDGE_GRAPH_LIMIT from 5000→500, add WebGL context-lost handlers in KnowledgeGraph.tsx.

Web UI auth system added in commit 009b1690. Cookie-based authentication with PBKDF2-SHA256 password hashing (no bcrypt dependency). Auth is optional — disabled when no username/password configured in DaemonConfig.auth. Key files: src/gobby/servers/middleware/auth.py (AuthMiddleware), src/gobby/servers/routes/auth.py (login/logout/status endpoints), src/gobby/storage/auth.py (session store + password hashing), auth_sessions table in migrations.py. Password stored via config secrets endpoint which hashes before storing.

React useCallback stale closure pattern in Gobby web UI: when an async handler inside useCallback references state (like graphData) and also appears in the dependency array, two problems occur: (1) the callback recreates on every state change causing unnecessary re-renders, and (2) if the async operation completes after state changes, it merges with stale data. Fix: use functional state updates — setGraphData(prev => prev ? mergeGraphData(prev, data) : data) — which removes the state from the dep array and always uses current state. Applied in KnowledgeGraph.tsx handleNodeClick.

KnowledgeGraph.tsx crash: fg.d3Force('charge').strength(-120) crashes when d3Force() returns undefined (force simulation not yet initialized). The 2D MemoryGraph already had optional chaining on these calls but the 3D KnowledgeGraph did not. Error occurs in useEffect so the KnowledgeGraphErrorBoundary doesn't catch it. Fix: add ?. to all three d3Force() calls at line 189. Also fixed stale graphData closure in handleNodeClick by using functional setGraphData(prev => ...) instead of capturing graphData in the dependency array.

The full Agent Definition Migration was completed across 3 sessions with 6 commits:
1. 16b94f1f - Rename 'generic' agent to 'default' (#8974)
2. 2d045d74 - Evolve AgentDefinitionBody schema with AgentWorkflows (#8982)
3. d24fb489 - Consolidate spawn_agent factory and delete _v2.py (#9023)
4. 0221d236 - Fresh agent YAMLs, simplify sync (#9023)
5. 4ccee279 - Delete AgentDefinition model and all conversion code (#9023)
6. 1df11664 - Add agent definition CRUD tools to gobby-agents MCP server (#9024)

Key architecture: AgentDefinitionBody (15 fields) is now the single source of truth. Old AgentDefinition (29 fields), WorkflowSpec, AgentDefinitionLoader all deleted. Agent defs stored in workflow_definitions table with workflow_type='agent'. Bundled YAML sync parses directly as AgentDefinitionBody. MCP tools for CRUD in agent_definitions.py.

Agent Definition Migration plan is COMPLETE (all 6 steps). The gobby-agents MCP server now has 5 agent definition CRUD tools: list_agent_definitions, get_agent_definition, create_agent_definition, toggle_agent_definition, delete_agent_definition. Implementation is in src/gobby/mcp_proxy/tools/agent_definitions.py following the same pattern as _rules.py. Tools are wired into create_agents_registry() in agents.py. All backed by LocalWorkflowDefinitionManager with workflow_type='agent'.

Agent Definition Architecture (post-migration, Feb 2026):

The old AgentDefinition model (29 fields, in agents/definitions.py) has been DELETED along with WorkflowSpec, SkillProfileConfig, AgentDefinitionLoader, AgentDefinitionInfo, and AgentSource.

The single source of truth is now AgentDefinitionBody (15 fields, in workflows/definitions.py) stored in workflow_definitions table with workflow_type='agent'. Key schema:
- AgentDefinitionBody: name, description, role, goal, personality, instructions, provider, model, mode, isolation, base_branch, timeout, max_turns, workflows (AgentWorkflows), enabled
- AgentWorkflows: pipeline (str|None), rules (list[str]), variables (dict)

Loading pattern: Use LocalWorkflowDefinitionManager.list_all(workflow_type='agent') then AgentDefinitionBody.model_validate_json(row.definition_json). No intermediate conversion.

Key code locations:
- Model: src/gobby/workflows/definitions.py (AgentDefinitionBody, AgentWorkflows)
- Sync: src/gobby/agents/sync.py (YAML → DB sync at startup)
- Factory: src/gobby/mcp_proxy/tools/spawn_agent/_factory.py (_load_agent_body)
- Routes: src/gobby/servers/routes/agents.py (CRUD + import/export)
- Bundled agents: src/gobby/install/shared/agents/ (5 YAML files)
- Old agents: src/gobby/install/shared/agents/deprecated/

Runner (agents/runner.py) no longer loads agent definitions — the factory handles all definition merging before spawn.

E2E test project ID leakage fix (commit 5842ff7c): Three root causes identified. (1) find_free_port() in tests/e2e/conftest.py was binding to localhost:0, which on macOS with SO_REUSEADDR could coexist with production daemon on 0.0.0.0 — fixed by binding to 0.0.0.0 and restricting to port range 30000-40000. (2) get_project_context() in src/gobby/utils/project_context.py unconditionally checked the contextvars.ContextVar before filesystem, even when explicit cwd was passed — fixed by gating the check on cwd being None. (3) No guard against test project IDs in production — added set_project_context() guard blocking 'e2e-test-project' and 'test-project' IDs unless GOBBY_TEST_PROTECT env var is set.

Bug: MCP proxy get_current_session resolved project_id to 'e2e-test-project' instead of the actual project. Session #1733 was not found in DB, task creation appeared to succeed (#9019) but the task was unfindable, and all FK-dependent MCP calls failed. Likely a test fixture or test DB leaking into the running daemon's project context.

Pre-existing issue: 14 DB-based tests in tests/workflows/test_task_enforcement_rules.py fail because _sync_bundled() + manager.get_by_name() returns None for all task-enforcement rules (block-native-task-tools, require-commit-before-close, block-skip-validation-with-commit, block-ask-during-stop-compliance, track-task-claim, track-task-release, and require-task-before-edit). The sync function doesn't find rules from the split file structure under task-enforcement/. Only condition evaluation tests (which don't use DB sync) pass.

The require-task-before-edit rule's plan file exemption was fixed in commit fd0dd823. Previously it required both plan_mode variable AND is_plan_file() to exempt plan files, but plan_mode depended on detect-plan-mode-enter/exit rules being enabled. Now is_plan_file() alone exempts plan files unconditionally. The is_plan_file() function (in gobby.workflows.enforcement.blocking) checks for .md files in .gobby/plans/ or .claude/plans/ directories.

Terminal context cleanup (for future work): Since tmux is the only terminal used, these can be removed from get_terminal_context() in hook_dispatcher.py: term_session_id (Terminal.app), iterm_session_id (iTerm2), kitty_window_id (Kitty), alacritty_socket (Alacritty), vscode_terminal_id (VS Code). Also clean up: TERMINAL_CONTEXT_KEYS in event_enrichment.py, terminal key loops in adapters (claude_code.py, gemini.py, codex_impl/adapter.py), and kill strategies in agents/registry.py (Kitty, Alacritty, iTerm strategies).

Tasks table dead weight analysis (for future cleanup):
- DEAD columns safe to drop: verification (set but never read), is_expanded (redundant with expansion_status), original_instruction (orphaned - in schema but not dataclass), details (orphaned), estimated_subtasks (written but never read)
- ALIVE columns that look dead but aren't: compacted_at/summary (used by compaction), expansion_context (used by expansion workflow), complexity_score (used in task selection scoring)
- Dead tables: session_memories (in schema but no code references), passkey_credentials/auth_sessions/auth_challenges (only in live DB, not in code)

Bug (FIXED in commit fd0dd823): require-task-before-edit rule previously blocked plan file writes because its condition depended on `plan_mode` variable (set by detect-plan-mode-enter/exit rules), but those rules are independently enabled and often disabled. Fix: removed the `plan_mode` guard so `is_plan_file()` is always evaluated; plan files are now always exempt from task-before-edit. File: src/gobby/install/shared/rules/task-enforcement/require-task-before-edit.yaml line 9.

SQLite column removal pattern used in migration v121: Since SQLite doesn't support DROP COLUMN on older versions, use table recreation: (1) fetchall existing rows, (2) DROP TABLE, (3) CREATE TABLE with new schema (without removed columns), (4) INSERT rows back selecting only kept columns. This pattern preserves data and FK constraints. Used to remove observations/reflection_pending from workflow_states.

Bug: gobby-memory search_memories can fail with 'Memory X not found' error when a deleted/stale memory ID is still referenced internally (possibly in cross-references or embeddings index). Observed when searching for 'observations reflection WorkflowState' — returned error referencing memory 55ce6532-9ac3-5f1b-9d1a-b1580ef125bc which no longer exists. Workaround: create a new memory instead of updating the stale one.

Observations system uses session variables, not WorkflowState fields. The `observe` rule effect (added in commit b2a3c375) appends structured entries to `_observations` session variable via RuleEngine. Context injection reads observations from SessionVariableManager.get_variables(session_id).get('_observations', []). Dead fields `observations` and `reflection_pending` were removed from WorkflowState model, state_manager SQL, and all callsites. Migration v121 drops the columns from workflow_states table.

Observations system: WorkflowState no longer has observations/reflection_pending fields (removed in migration v121). Observations are now stored in the _observations session variable via SessionVariableManager. The 'observe' rule effect (5th type after block/set_variable/inject_context/mcp_call) appends structured entries {category, message, timestamp, rule} to _observations in the variables dict during rule evaluation. Context injection reads observations from session_variable_manager.get_variables(session_id).get('_observations', []). The handle_inject_context wrapper creates SessionVariableManager(context.db) and passes it to inject_context.

All CLI adapters (Claude Code, Gemini, Cursor, Windsurf, Copilot, Codex) nest the Bash command inside event.data.tool_input.command, NOT at event.data.command. Any code in rule_engine.py that reads command from event.data must also check tool_input as fallback. Fixed in _should_block() (commit 80a52a8a).

tests/workflows/test_task_enforcement_rules.py has 16 pre-existing failures: _sync_bundled(db) + manager.get_by_name() returns None for all task-enforcement rule names (block-native-task-tools, require-task-before-edit, etc.). The bundled rule sync mechanism changed but these tests weren't updated. Tracked as #9011.

is_plan_file() is a condition helper for the rule engine, defined in src/gobby/workflows/enforcement/blocking.py. It checks if a file path is in .gobby/plans/ or ~/.claude/plans/ and ends with .md. Registered as an allowed_func in rule_engine.py _evaluate_condition(). Used by require-task-before-edit rule to unconditionally exempt plan file edits without a claimed task (no longer requires plan_mode variable after commit fd0dd823).

- tests/workflows/test_memory_lifecycle_rules.py: 20 failures, _sync_bundled(db) returns None for rule lookups (#8998)
- tests/workflows/test_coordinator_pipeline.py: 10 failed + 23 errors, developer.yaml/coordinator.yaml removed (#8979)

When sessions crash during workflow engine refactoring, new sessions often pick up the same work and commit under new task IDs, leaving original tasks stale in in_progress state. To triage: cross-reference git log task ID tags against open task list, run the actual tests mentioned in bug tasks to check if issues still reproduce, then close as already_implemented (with commit_sha from the actual fix commit) or duplicate. close_task with reason='already_implemented' or 'duplicate' auto-skips validation but still needs commit_sha if the original session had edits.

Pre-existing test failures found 2026-02-23: (1) test_toggle_rule_rejects_template in test_rule_engine.py - toggle_rule incorrectly returns success=True for template-only rules (filed #9009). (2) 20 failures in test_progressive_disclosure_rules.py - rule lookups return None and integration tests fail to block, likely caused by removed/renamed progressive disclosure rule templates (filed #9010).

RuleEngine._evaluate_condition (rule_engine.py) now uses build_condition_helpers() from safe_evaluator.py as the canonical source of allowed_funcs, with rule-engine-specific helpers (is_server_listed, is_tool_unlocked, is_discovery_tool, isinstance) layered on top. Any new helpers added to build_condition_helpers() will automatically be available in rule conditions.

ConversationPicker component (Chats sidebar) is rendered in ChatPage.tsx, which receives a ConversationState prop from App.tsx. To add new props: update ConversationPickerProps interface, ConversationState in types/chat.ts, the conversations object in App.tsx (~line 422), and the prop pass-through in ChatPage.tsx (~line 46).

TasksPage.tsx uses both localStats (computed from client-side max 200 tasks) and server-side stats from useTasks(). The TaskOverview component should use server-side stats for correct global counts. The localStats is still used in the filter chips bar to show counts of visible tasks. Default task filter should be 'open' (useTasks.ts:134) to avoid closed tasks dominating the view.

Web UI CSS architecture: Chat sidebar uses var(--bg-tertiary) for background and calc(var(--font-size-base) * 0.85) for title font-size. Terminals sidebar should match these values. The conversation-picker styles are at index.css:1869+, terminals-sidebar styles at index.css:2897+. Agent card styles are at index.css:11355+.

The deprecated session-lifecycle.yaml workflow used action-based enforcement (block_tools, track_schema_lookup, track_discovery_step actions registered in actions.py). These were removed in #8980. The new rule engine uses standalone YAML rule files with effects (type: block) evaluated directly in rule_engine.py. Key functions kept in blocking.py: DISCOVERY_TOOLS, is_discovery_tool(), is_tool_unlocked(), is_server_listed(), is_plan_file(). The rule engine supports command_pattern/command_not_pattern natively (rule_engine.py:309-327). Note: is_plan_file() was added to the rule engine's allowed_funcs as part of #9003.

hook_manager.handle() execution order: For SESSION_START events, the handler must run BEFORE workflow rules evaluate. This is because the session handler registers the session and sets event.metadata['_platform_session_id']. Without this, WorkflowHookHandler._evaluate_rules() falls back to event.session_id (the CLI's external_id) at hooks.py:78, causing set_variable effects to be stored under the wrong key. For all other events, rules evaluate first so block effects can prevent handler execution. Fixed in commit dcb6af09.

WorkflowHookHandler.handle() now delegates directly to evaluate() (rule engine). The step-based workflow engine (engine.handle_event) is dead code — 0 workflows in DB use it. Before this fix, handle() ran evaluate() then ran the dead engine and returned its empty response, discarding rule metadata including mcp_call effects. This caused title synthesis and other mcp_call-based features to silently fail.

Event handlers (_agent.py, _tool.py, _session.py) no longer call _evaluate_workflows() — rules are evaluated once in hook_manager.handle() via workflow_handler.handle(), not twice. The _apply_debug_echo() method no longer takes a wf_response parameter; it reads debug_echo_context from WorkflowConfig only.

Hook system architecture: Rules evaluate ONCE centrally in hook_manager.handle() via workflow_handler.handle() -> evaluate(). Event handlers (_agent.py, _tool.py, _session.py) do NOT evaluate rules themselves. Before #8994 fix, rules were evaluated twice (once in hook_manager, once in each handler via _evaluate_workflows), and the central path discarded rule metadata (including mcp_calls) by running the dead step-based engine after rule evaluation. The fix made handle() return evaluate() directly, preserving mcp_call metadata for dispatch. The _evaluate_workflows method was removed from EventHandlersBase.

Removed dead code fields from workflow definitions: WorkflowStep.check_rules (list[str]) and WorkflowDefinition.on_error (list[dict[str, Any]]). Neither field was ever read by production code. check_rules was declared at definitions.py:221 and on_error at definitions.py:276. The step workflow engine (engine.py + unified_evaluator.py) is legacy code slated for removal per docs/plans/workflows-v2.md Phase 6. step.rules IS referenced in unified_evaluator.py:203 (separate from check_rules). step.on_mcp_error IS used by engine.py:641 (separate from on_error). Also cleaned up deprecated/developer.yaml which had check_rules entries.

DevMode Install button: All 3 workflow tabs (PipelinesTab, AgentsTab, RulesTab) now show an Install button in the devMode branch for template cards, matching the non-devMode behavior. Previously devMode hid the Install button, leaving only YAML/Edit/Duplicate/Delete actions.

Sync template restoration policy: sync_bundled_rules and sync_bundled_workflows now restore soft-deleted templates instead of skipping them. Templates should always be available — if users don't want one, they disable it. Restored templates are set to enabled=False. Also: when an installed copy shadows a template and the template row is missing or soft-deleted, the sync now creates/restores the template row (previously it silently skipped). The template row query was widened to include soft-deleted rows (removed `AND deleted_at IS NULL`).

WorkflowsPage tab architecture: All 3 tabs (Pipelines, Agents, Rules) are now extracted into consistent tab components: PipelinesTab.tsx, AgentsTab.tsx, RulesTab.tsx. WorkflowsPage.tsx is a thin shell (~170 lines) with toolbar, TabBar, and tab routing. YamlEditorModal is exported from WorkflowsPage and shared by all tabs. Each tab receives searchText, sourceFilter, devMode, refreshKey props. Create buttons stay in the toolbar; create UI (forms/modals/dropdowns) renders inside each tab via showCreate* props.

When using get_by_name for rules/workflows that may have both template and installed copies with the same name, use the fallback pattern: `get_by_name(name) or get_by_name(name, include_templates=True)`. This prefers installed copies (which the user interacts with) and only falls back to templates when no installed copy exists. Do NOT use include_templates=True directly as it may return the template instead of the installed copy, breaking toggle/YAML operations.

The require-uv hook rule is producing false positives - it blocks ALL Bash commands including those that already use 'uv run'. This was encountered in session #1711 and prevented running tests. The recent commit f21ed616 (bare variable false positives fix) may need a daemon restart to take effect, or there may be an additional bug in the rule.

Renamed use_as_template → install_from_template and use_all_bundled_as_templates → install_all_templates across the entire codebase. Routes changed: /use-as-template → /install, /use-all-bundled-as-templates → /install-all-templates. Also fixed a bug where install_from_template hardcoded enabled=True instead of preserving original.enabled. Frontend components (RulesTab, WorkflowsPage, AgentDefinitionsPage) and hooks (useRules, useWorkflows) were all updated. Button labels changed from 'Use as Template' to 'Install'.

RuleEffect.mcp_tools field is list[str] with format 'server:tool' (e.g., 'gobby-tasks:close_task') or 'server:*' for wildcards. It is NOT a list of dicts. The rule engine matches mcp_tools against event.data['mcp_tool'] and event.data['mcp_server']. For call_tool events, _build_eval_context() unwraps the nested arguments dict so rule conditions like tool_input.get('commit_sha') resolve against inner tool params.

Rule engine block messages now include the rule name. In rule_engine.py line 119, after Jinja rendering, block_reason is prepended with 'Rule enforced by Gobby: [{_row.name}]\n'. This means all hook error messages from rule blocks show which rule fired. The change is in the single code path where block effects are applied (the evaluate() method's block handling loop).

Confirmed dead code in workflow definitions (session #1699 research): (1) WorkflowState.observations - REMOVED in migration v121 (was initialized to [] but never appended to; now reimplemented as _observations session variable), (2) WorkflowState.reflection_pending - REMOVED in migration v121 (was always False, never set True; now reimplemented via observe rule effect), (3) WorkflowStep.check_rules - field declared but no code reads it (step.rules IS used, check_rules is separate dead field), (4) WorkflowStep.on_error - declared but engine uses on_mcp_error instead. NOT dead (research agents were wrong): session_variables on WorkflowDefinition, task_list/current_task_index/files_modified_this_task, exit_when/exit_conditions, on_premature_stop, step.rules.

Agent definition redesign vision (task #8982): Restructure AgentDefinition.workflows from dict[str, WorkflowSpec] to a flat struct with: workflows.pipeline (one pipeline name, DB-backed not file), workflows.rules (list of rule names), workflows.variables (pre-seed session vars, override rule defaults). Pipeline auto-start is configurable. YAML files are templates, DB is source of truth. Agents become orchestration containers. Depends on generic→default rename (#8974) and variable layer cleanup.

Variable architecture has 3 scopes: (1) WorkflowState.variables (legacy, per-session single workflow, in workflow_states table), (2) WorkflowInstance.variables (per-workflow per-session, in workflow_instances table), (3) SessionVariableManager (shared across all workflows, in session_variables table, newest/preferred). Rules use SessionVariableManager via set_variable effects. Pipelines have their own separate input/output data flow and don't use workflow state variables at all.

task_ref workflow variable: Set to `#{seq_num}` (e.g., `#8978`) when claiming/creating a task, cleared to `""` when closing. Previously used in the require-task-close rule template `{{ task_ref }}`, but that was a bug (task_ref is not in the rule engine's eval context). The template now uses `{{ claimed_task_id[:8] if claimed_task_id else '(unknown)' }}` instead. task_ref may still be used elsewhere (e.g., commit_policy.py context). Must be initialized to `""` via init-task-ref.yaml to prevent empty rendering. Similarly, task_claimed must be initialized to `false` via init-task-claimed.yaml to prevent NameError in the `when` condition.

Rule engine `when` conditions that use bare variable names (e.g., `task_claimed`) instead of `variables.get('task_claimed')` will raise NameError via SafeExpressionEvaluator when the variable is undefined. For `block` effects, this fails closed (always blocks). All rule variables used in bare form must have corresponding init rules in `session-defaults/` that set defaults on `session_start`. The `installed` source in workflow_definitions is a copy of a `template` row — when sync.py updates a template's definition_json, it must also propagate to the installed copy via `_propagate_to_installed()`.

Rule engine mcp_call effects are dispatched by HookManager._dispatch_mcp_calls() (added in ddc4aa7c). The rule engine builds mcp_calls into HookResponse.metadata['mcp_calls'] but does NOT dispatch them itself — HookManager.handle() extracts them after workflow evaluation and dispatches via ToolProxyService. Context injection: session_id comes from event.metadata['_platform_session_id'], prompt_text from event.data['prompt']. Background calls use asyncio fire-and-forget pattern matching broadcasting.

Web UI project selection architecture: The app-level project selector in App.tsx provides `effectiveProjectId` which should be passed as a prop to all tab pages that need project scoping. Pages should NOT manage their own projectId state — this causes the backend `_resolve_project` to fall back to the first project alphabetically. The pattern is: App.tsx passes `projectId={effectiveProjectId}` to page components, which forward it to their data hooks (e.g., `useSourceControl(projectId)`). Fixed in commit 3b459a62 for GitHubPage.

User preference: workflow filter UI should use a dropdown/select element, not chips/buttons. Chips take up too much space. The dropdown should match the height of the search box (same padding, font-size, border styles).

Bundled rules split from 11 group YAML files into ~80 individual files in group subdirectories under src/gobby/install/shared/rules/<group-name>/. Old group files moved to deprecated/. Individual files use tags: [group-name] instead of group: field. sync_bundled_rules() skips deprecated/ via path filter and has orphan cleanup for removed rules.

Source values renamed in workflow_definitions table: 'bundled' → 'template', 'custom' → 'installed'. Migration 120 handles existing DB rows. Backward-compat SQL uses IN ('bundled', 'template') in sync.py for transition period. Prompts scope='bundled' is a different concept and was NOT renamed. Skills 'bundled' stat also left unchanged.

Rules in src/gobby/install/shared/rules/ are templates with enabled: false by default. They get synced/installed to the database where they're actually enabled. Don't modify the install folder to enable rules — they're already installed and enabled in the DB.

Don't use skip_validation on close_task unless there's a genuine reason (e.g. validator can't see the diff, truncated context). Tasks without validation_criteria just need a linked commit_sha to close normally. Using skip_validation unnecessarily routes tasks to needs_review instead of closing them.

Rule group derivation: After the per-file YAML restructure (one rule per file in subdirectories), the group field is derived from the subdirectory name during sync_bundled_rules(). E.g., task-enforcement/require-task-before-edit.yaml → group: 'task-enforcement'. Explicit group: in YAML takes precedence. This powers list_rules_by_group(), /api/rules/groups, and CLI rules export.

Rules activation mechanism: Bundled rules sync as source='template' and are excluded from rule engine evaluation (list_rules_by_event filters source != 'template'). To activate, rules must be 'installed' via install_from_template() or install_all_templates() which creates source='installed' copies. This is the intended design — templates are browsable/installable but don't fire until explicitly activated. The list_rules_by_event and list_rules_by_group queries both filter out template source.
In the gobby project, expansion operations should use the opus model (claude-opus-4-6) rather than sonnet. This is configured in gobby-config and was identified as needing a fix.
If Antigravity gets stuck on Fail/Failure to Send, switch the model to Gemini Flash and send a prompt. Then you can switch back to Gemini Pro.
Gobby's create_task API has a bug where the depends_on parameter is inverted — specifying depends_on creates the dependency relationship in the wrong direction. Filed as bug #8929 with priority 1.

In gobby's windsurf.py, the `uninstall_windsurf()` function (line 179+) has a bug at line 199 in the global mode branch (`mode == 'g...'`). In gobby's test suite, Click's CliRunner captures all stdout/stderr, so print-based debugging in CLI commands (e.g., install.py) won't produce visible output during tests. To debug, check `result.output` from the CliRunner invocation or write debug info to a temp file instead.

When debugging 'button not working' in the web UI, always check if the daemon needs restarting to pick up new backend code. New API routes won't be available until the daemon is restarted (uv run gobby restart). Test endpoints via curl before assuming frontend bugs. Also, zsh interprets ? in URLs - always quote curl URLs.

workflow_definitions table has a unique index idx_wf_defs_name_project on (name, COALESCE(project_id, '__global__'), source). Without source in the index, creating a custom copy from a bundled template fails with UNIQUE constraint violation. Rule engine variable persistence architecture: The rule engine (RuleEngine.evaluate) mutates a variables dict in-place via set_variable effects, but the engine itself is stateless — it doesn't persist anything. The integration point is WorkflowHookHandler._evaluate_rules() in src/gobby/workflows/hooks.py, which is responsible for loading variables (from both workflow_states AND session_variables tables, with session_variables taking precedence) and persisting changes back after evaluation. There are THREE separate variable stores: (1) workflow_states.variables (legacy workflow engine state), (2) session_variables (session-scoped shared variables, written by MCP set_session_variable), (3) workflow_instances.variables (per-workflow-instance). The rule engine reads from both #1 and #2 merged, and writes back changes to #2. Fixed in commit 0c563945.

Stop-gate before_agent resets must NOT reset stop_attempts or other stop-cycle state unconditionally. When a stop hook blocks, Claude Code fires before_agent for the feedback message, creating a cycle. Fix (commit 6a9f2f9d): removed reset-stop-attempts-on-prompt rule entirely (stop_attempts only resets via after_tool on native tool use), and gated clear-tool-block-on-prompt and reset-error-triage-on-prompt with when: stop_attempts==0 so they only fire on genuine new prompts, not stop-cycle feedback.

Investigation: Expander Agent Replacing Task Decomposition (#8943)

## Key Finding: Expansion is Already Skill-Driven

The old expansion.py (TaskExpander) has been REMOVED. Current architecture:
1. Skill (expand/SKILL.md, 458 lines): Instructs AI agent to analyze codebase, generate spec, create subtasks
2. MCP Tools (_expansion.py): save_expansion_spec, execute_expansion, get_expansion_spec
3. Flow: Agent reads task -> analyzes codebase (Glob/Grep/Read, visible) -> generates spec -> saves -> executes atomically
4. Compaction recovery: save_expansion_spec persists spec to DB, get_expansion_spec detects pending for resume

## Assessment: Dedicated Agent Has Questionable Value

Current skill approach advantages:
- Conversation visible to user (transparency, debugging)
- Uses existing session context (codebase knowledge)
- No spawn latency
- Interactive (user can guide decomposition)
- Session compaction recovery via save/resume

Dedicated agent approach would add:
- Isolated context (won't pollute session) 
- Parallel execution capability
- But: loses interactivity, visibility, existing context, adds spawn latency

## Recommendation: Keep Skill-Based, Enhance with Rules

Rather than spawning a dedicated agent, add expansion quality rules:
- before_tool on save_expansion_spec: validate subtask count (warn if <2 or >15)
- Validate description length (warn if any subtask <50 chars)
- Validate TDD compliance (check code/config tasks have TDD headers)
- Validate dependency graph (warn on circular deps)

This gives enforcement benefits without dedicated agent complexity. The expansion is already agent-driven — the skill IS the agent behavior, just running in the current session.

Feasibility of dedicated agent: MODERATE technically, LOW value proposition.

In the gobby project, a pre-tool-use hook (hook_dispatcher.py) blocks Write operations unless the agent has an active/claimed task. You must first create and claim a task via the gobby-tasks MCP server (using create_task) before any file writes will be permitted. The agent spent ~10 turns discovering this workflow.

gobby's chat_session.py was 1,110 lines and had permission-related code extracted into chat_session_permissions.py at src/gobby/servers/chat_session_permissions.py as part of a decomposition to get under a 1,000-line guideline.

In the gobby project, the hook_dispatcher.py pre-tool-use hook enforces file-level write permissions. When writing new files during a decomposition, the first Write to create a new file may succeed but subsequent Writes (e.g., updating the original file) can be blocked with a 'You must claim' error. The agent needed to use mcp__gobby__call_tool to claim files before writing, and even that call can be blocked by the same hook system, requiring multiple retry strategies.

After bulk-modifying gobby's SQLite database (e.g., updating session_variables), the workflow cache must be explicitly cleared via the MCP tool (mcp__gobby_ prefixed) for changes to take effect in workflow evaluations.

In gobby's SQLite database, the session_variables table stores variable values with a separate 'type' column (e.g., 'integer', 'string'). When updating boolean-like variables such as debug_echo_context, the value is stored as integer 0/1, not as JSON boolean true/false. Direct SQL updates require matching this format.

Investigation: Developer Agent TDD Enforcement via Rules (#8942)

## Current TDD Enforcement Gaps

1. Task expansion creates TDD headers in descriptions (prompt-level only, no runtime enforcement)
2. Developer workflow (developer.yaml) has red/green/blue steps with tests_written/tests_passing variables, BUT agent self-reports via set_workflow_variable — no verification
3. Red phase has 'test_files_only' rule with action:warn (not block) — agent can ignore
4. Green phase has edit_count_since_test_run reminder (also warn) — agent can ignore
5. All enforcement is step-based (requires explicit workflow activation), not always-on rules

## Proposed Rule-Based TDD Enforcement

New rule group 'tdd-enforcement' with agent_scope: [developer]:
- track-test-writes: after_tool → set_variable tests_written_in_session when Edit/Write touches test file
- require-tests-first: before_tool → block Edit/Write on src/ if no test file modified yet
- track-test-runs: after_tool → set_variable last_test_run_step when Bash runs pytest
- track-edit-count: after_tool → increment edit_count on each Edit/Write
- test-run-reminder: before_agent → inject_context when 5+ edits without test run
- require-tests-before-commit: before_tool → block git commit if no tests run in session

## Required Helper Functions for SafeExpressionEvaluator
- is_test_file(path) — matches tests/, test_*, *_test.py patterns
- is_src_file(path) — matches src/ (not tests/)
- contains_test_command(cmd) — matches pytest/unittest in bash command

## Edge Cases
- Test-only tasks (category: test): skip test-first enforcement
- Config tasks: TDD applies
- Docs tasks: no TDD
- Refactoring: require test runs but not test-first writes
- Solution: condition checks variables.get('task_category') to gate enforcement

## Feasibility: HIGH
Rule infrastructure supports this. Only missing: helper functions for file path classification in SafeExpressionEvaluator. Implementation path: add helpers, create YAML rule group, wire task category as session variable on claim_task, test with developer agents, eventually deprecate step-based red/green/blue.

Investigation: QA Agent Replacing TaskValidator (#8941)

Current TaskValidator flow: validation.py gathers context (uncommitted changes, multi-commit window, file-based analysis), builds LLM prompt via PromptLoader, calls provider, parses JSON {status, feedback}. External validator (external_validator.py) has 3 modes: 'llm' (fresh API call), 'agent' (in-process AgentRunner), 'spawn' (headless agent). Returns ExternalValidationResult with structured issues. Close task integration: validate_commit_requirements -> validate_parent_task -> gather_validation_context -> validate_leaf_task_with_llm -> determine_close_outcome.

Feasibility: HIGH. Infrastructure already supports QA agent pattern - external_validator has agent/spawn modes, qa-reviewer workflow exists, agent-scoped rules implemented, close_task routes to external validation.

Recommended approach: Evolve external_validator spawn mode rather than replacing TaskValidator. Keep internal validator as fast path. Create qa-reviewer agent definition with rules (block edits/writes, inject task context, enforce structured output). Configure external_validator_mode: 'spawn' with agent: 'qa-reviewer'. Integrates via existing run_external_validation -> _run_spawn_validation path.

Advantages: tool access (read files, run tests), multi-turn reasoning, extensible via rules. Challenges: latency (30-60s vs 5-10s), higher token cost, less deterministic.

MCP action tool wrapping pattern: To expose workflow actions as MCP tools, create thin wrappers using @registry.tool() decorator in the relevant registry module. Pattern: (1) Import the workflow action function, (2) Create async wrapper that captures managers via closure, (3) Handle None manager with early error return, (4) Call action, check for errors, return {success: True, **result}. For new tool groups, create a register_*_tools() function in a separate _actions.py file and call it from the _factory.py. Wire new dependencies through registries.py setup_internal_registries(). See sessions/_actions.py and memory.py lines 606-678 for examples.

MCP tool registration pattern: Factory functions (create_*_registry) return InternalToolRegistry. Tools registered via @registry.tool() decorator. Closures over manager params provide dependency injection. Both sync and async tools supported. Registries merged in registries.py via setup_internal_registries(). For session tools, use sub-modules (e.g., _actions.py) and register_*_tools() functions wired in _factory.py.

RuleEngine implementation (task #8806): The core RuleEngine is in src/gobby/workflows/rule_engine.py. It maps HookEventType→RuleEvent, loads rules via LocalWorkflowDefinitionManager.list_rules_by_event(), applies session overrides from rule_overrides table, and evaluates in priority order. Five effect types: block (with tool/mcp/command matching), set_variable (with expression evaluation via SafeExpressionEvaluator.evaluate_value()), inject_context (template accumulation), mcp_call (records in metadata), and observe (appends structured entries to _observations in variables dict). The SafeExpressionEvaluator was extended with evaluate_value() for non-bool returns and visit_BinOp for arithmetic (+, -, *, //, %). Key design: _should_block handles native tools, MCP tools (server:tool pattern with wildcard support), and command patterns. Block is first-match-wins (breaks loop). Other effects accumulate.

The gobby web UI has a split styling approach: the chat UI (web/src/components/chat/) uses Tailwind CSS with its own styles.css importing tailwindcss, while all other pages (terminals, sessions, memory, etc.) use regular CSS in web/src/styles/index.css with CSS custom properties (--font-size-base: 16px, --font-mono, --font-sans, etc.). When matching styles between chat and non-chat components, map Tailwind classes to their CSS equivalents (e.g., text-xs = 0.75rem, py-1.5 = 0.375rem, px-4 = 1rem).

To deploy frontend changes in gobby: run `npm run build` in the web/ directory (Vite build), then `uv run gobby restart` to restart the daemon which serves the built static files. Both steps are needed - the build generates new JS/CSS bundles, and the daemon restart picks up both new static assets and any Python backend changes. Users need to hard-refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) to get the new bundle.

The memory stats endpoint (GET /memories/stats) now includes `recent_count` - the number of memories created in the last 24 hours. This was added to `src/gobby/memory/services/maintenance.py` get_stats() and exposed in the MemoryStats TypeScript type. The frontend 24H chip in MemoryFilters uses this server-side count for accuracy.

Memory page MemoryOverview cards showed inaccurate counts because they computed from the `memories` array (fetched with limit:100) while the MemoryFilters chips used `stats.by_type` from the full DB stats API. This caused confusing mismatches (e.g., 39 vs 386 for Facts). Fix: removed the cards entirely and kept the filter chips which use accurate server-side stats.

Bug pattern: add_dependency(task_id, depends_on) uses positional args where task_id is the dependent and depends_on is the blocker. When calling from create_task's blocks/depends_on handlers, it's easy to swap these. The expansion code (_expansion.py) avoided this by using named parameters (task_id=..., depends_on=...). Lesson: always use named parameters for add_dependency calls to make the semantic meaning clear and prevent arg-swap bugs.

Gobby's create_task has a bug (#8929) where the depends_on parameter is inverted — dependencies are stored/applied in the wrong direction. When task A is created with depends_on=[task_B], the relationship is recorded backwards.

BUG: The gobby-tasks `create_task` tool's `depends_on` parameter creates REVERSED dependencies. When you pass `depends_on: ["#X"]` to create task #Y, it creates "#X depends on #Y" instead of the intended "#Y depends on #X". Workaround: Do NOT use `depends_on` in create_task. Instead, create the task first, then use `add_dependency(task_id="#Y", depends_on="#X")` which works correctly. Discovered in session #1665 when creating Phase 2/3 canvas tasks.

When running `npx tsc` or other commands via `cd /path && command`, the CWD persists for subsequent Bash calls. This means a later `git add web/src/...` fails with 'web/web/src' path duplication because CWD is still in web/. Always use absolute paths or explicit `cd /project/root &&` prefix for git operations after any cd into a subdirectory.

File decomposition patterns used to bring 4 files under 1,000 lines:

1. **Package extraction** (spawn_agent.py 1016→5 files, max 540): Convert single file to package with __init__.py re-exports. Best when file has 4+ distinct responsibility groups. Follow existing pattern (tasks/ was already a package).

2. **Module-level function extraction** (runner.py 1068→807 + 2 modules): Extract groups of standalone methods into separate modules as functions. Used runner_broadcasting.py (160 lines) and runner_maintenance.py (120 lines). Works when methods are self-contained utilities called during init.

3. **Mixin extraction** (chat.py 1153→774 + session_control.py 439): Extract methods into a new mixin class, add to WebSocket server's inheritance chain. Follow existing mixin pattern (HandlerMixin, AuthMixin, etc.). Use TYPE_CHECKING for cross-mixin method references.

4. **Helper deduplication + setup extraction** (install.py 1114→942 + install_setup.py 119): Extract repeated output formatting into helper functions (_echo_install_details, _echo_uninstall_details). Move setup logic to separate module. Use re-export for backward compatibility with test patches: `_ensure_daemon_config = ensure_daemon_config`.

Key lesson: When moving functions that are patched in tests, either (a) keep the call site in the original module so patches still work, or (b) update all test patch targets. Option (a) is safer when there are many patches.

In the gobby project, a pre-tool-use hook (`~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py`) blocks Write operations with 'You must create a task first' error. The workaround is to create a task via the gobby-tasks MCP server (using `mcp__gobby_tasks__create_task`) before attempting any file writes.

In the gobby project, a pre-tool-use hook (`~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py`) blocks Write operations with 'You must create a task first' error. The workaround is to create a task via the gobby-tasks MCP server (using `mcp__gobby_tasks__create_task`) before attempting any file writes.

In the gobby project, a pre-tool-use hook at ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py blocks Write operations unless a gobby task has been created and claimed first. The workflow requires: (1) discover gobby-tasks MCP server tools, (2) call mcp__gobby_tasks__create_task to create a task, (3) claim the task, then (4) proceed with file writes.

In the gobby project, a pre-tool-use hook at ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py blocks Write operations unless a gobby task has been created and claimed first. The workflow requires: (1) discover gobby-tasks MCP server tools, (2) call mcp__gobby_tasks__create_task to create a task, (3) claim the task, then (4) proceed with file writes.
In the gobby project, task expansion should use Claude Opus (not Sonnet) as the model. This needs to be configured in gobby-config. As of 2026-02-21 it was still using Sonnet for expansion.

Daemon startup performance: export_to_jsonl() was the bottleneck (~63s) because _normalize_commits() spawned git rev-parse --short per commit SHA (~3848 subprocesses). This was redundant since link_commit() in _lifecycle.py already normalizes SHAs at write time. Fix: replaced with sorted(set(task.commits)). The install_default_mcp_servers() function in cli/installers/mcp_config.py only adds new servers but never repairs existing misconfigured ones — added a transport reconciliation step that detects when an existing server's transport diverges from DEFAULT_MCP_SERVERS and auto-fixes it. VectorStore.count() is async-only, but get_stats() is sync and runs inside the daemon's event loop. Added count_sync() to VectorStore for sync callers, avoiding the asyncio.run()-inside-running-loop problem that produced thousands of RuntimeWarnings.

In plan mode, Bash mkdir is blocked by _BASH_WRITE_PATTERNS, but the Write tool auto-creates parent directories. So writing to .gobby/plans/foo.md works even if .gobby/plans/ doesn't exist yet — no need to mkdir first. The _PLAN_FILE_PATTERN regex (chat_session_helpers.py) exempts Write/Edit to .claude/plans/*.md and .gobby/plans/*.md from plan mode blocking.

Plan mode artifact display gap: The artifact system (useArtifacts/ArtifactPanel) and plan mode (PlanApprovalBar) are completely disconnected. tool_status WebSocket events for Write tool calls include full arguments (file_path + content), so plan file writes can be detected purely on the frontend by matching file_path against the plan file regex. No backend changes needed. Implementation plan at .gobby/plans/plan-artifact-display.md.

The `gobby uninstall` command has an interactive confirmation prompt ('Are you sure you want to uninstall Gobby hooks? [y/N]') that defaults to No and aborts when run non-interactively. Must pipe `yes | gobby uninstall` to bypass it in automated/agent contexts.

Gobby's Knowledge Graph / 3D memory visualization requires Neo4j running via Docker (container name starts with 'neo4j-'). Neo4j is mapped to port 8474. If the memory page shows no graph, first check: 1) Docker is running, 2) Neo4j container is up ('docker ps'), 3) port 8474 responds.

In gobby, the backend /admin/status endpoint reports Neo4j as 'configured: true' even when Neo4j is not actually running or reachable. To verify Neo4j is truly available, check port 8474 directly (non-standard port, not the default 7474).

OpenClaw teaches agents about canvas tools via a Skill system (skills/canvas/SKILL.md) — YAML frontmatter + markdown body injected into agent context. The skill provides procedural step-by-step instructions, not just API reference. Key lesson: agents need to be explicitly taught when/how to use new tools through skills, not just tool descriptions. Tool descriptions tell agents a tool exists; skills teach them how and when to use it effectively.

The skill injection system (src/gobby/skills/injector.py) currently filters by audience (agent_type), depth, workflow steps, and task_categories — but NOT by session source (CLI vs web). AgentContext.source is populated from the session and available at injection time, but SkillAudienceConfig has no sources field. The plan adds a `sources` field to SkillAudienceConfig and a source check in _matches_audience(). This enables skills like canvas to use alwaysApply:true + sources:["claude_sdk_web_chat"] to auto-inject only for web UI sessions.

A2UI (Agent-to-User Interface) is a standalone Google project (github.com/google/A2UI, Apache 2.0, v0.8 public preview). It's a declarative JSON format where agents describe UI components from a catalog (Text, Button, TextField, CheckBox, Row, Column, Card, List, Image, Icon, Badge). Messages are JSONL: surfaceUpdate, dataModelUpdate, beginRendering, deleteSurface. User interactions return as userAction messages. OpenClaw vendors it. CopilotKit's React renderer is tightly coupled to their SDK and not reusable. No standalone React renderer exists — we need to build our own. The revised canvas plan is at docs/plans/a2ui-canvas-v2.md.

.gobby/tasks_meta.json causes merge conflicts on every branch merge because it contains content_hash (differs per branch's task state) and last_exported (differs per export timestamp). The file is a local cache used only to skip redundant exports in TaskSyncManager.export_to_jsonl (src/gobby/sync/tasks.py:239). It should be added to .gitignore.

In gobby installer tests (e.g., test_gemini_installer.py), GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR values in test assertions must match the mocked environment/paths. When tests fail with path mismatches, the root cause is typically the test's expected GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR not aligning with what the mock setup provides. There are 307 total installer tests across all installer test files.

User preference: Do not include arbitrary human-time estimates (weeks, months) in plans. When agents are building, time estimates based on human developer speed are meaningless. Frame plans around dependency order and parallelizability instead.

A Go CLI strangler fig migration feasibility study was completed and written to docs/research/go-cli-strangler-fig.md (#8893). Key findings: ~60 thin client commands (HTTP to daemon) are trivial to port, ~30 local logic commands need real reimplementation. install/uninstall is the hardest (~1000 LOC). The CLI communicates with the daemon via DaemonClient (src/gobby/utils/daemon_client.py) using httpx to localhost:60887. Phase 2 (thin clients) and Phase 3 (local logic) can run in parallel — both only depend on the Phase 1 scaffold. The user prefers not to see arbitrary human-time estimates in plans when agents will do the work.

When installer tests mock Path.home() for isolation, they must ALSO set GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR to match (e.g. mock_home/.gobby/hooks). Otherwise protect_production_resources sets GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR to a different temp dir, and installers that read GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR (codex, gemini via install_global_hooks) write hooks to the wrong location, causing assertion failures. Pattern: patch.dict(os.environ, {"GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR": str(mock_home / ".gobby" / "hooks")})

pytest coverage (--cov=gobby) was removed from pyproject.toml addopts to avoid 10-30% overhead on every local test run. Coverage is now only enabled explicitly in CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml) and pre-push-test.sh. To run locally with coverage: uv run pytest tests/path/ --cov=gobby --cov-report=term-missing

The protect_production_resources fixture in tests/conftest.py previously scanned ALL sys.modules on every test setup/teardown to find 'rogue references' to load_config/save_config. Only 7 modules actually do top-level imports: gobby.config, gobby.runner, gobby.cli, gobby.cli.utils, gobby.cli.tasks._utils, gobby.mcp_proxy.stdio, gobby.watchdog. All others use lazy (in-function) imports. The scan was replaced with a hardcoded _KNOWN_CONFIG_IMPORTERS list.

When mocking subprocess.run side_effect for WorktreeGitManager.merge_branch tests, the finally block always runs checkout to restore the original branch when checked_out_target=True and original_branch != target_branch. This means successful merge tests with push=True need 7 mocks (rev-parse, fetch, checkout, pull, merge, push, checkout-restore), not 6. The StopIteration error from mock side_effect running out of entries is the symptom.

When gobby's hook_dispatcher.py is accidentally deleted during a Claude Code session, Edit tool calls fail with PreToolUse hook errors. The workaround is to use the MCP gobby tool (mcp__gobby_*) to restore the hooks before retrying the Edit.

In the gobby project, uninstall tests can delete the real ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py file because they aren't properly isolated from the real global hooks directory. This causes Claude Code PreToolUse:Edit hooks to fail mid-session with error 'You must...' when the dispatcher is missing. The root cause is the global uninstall code in install.py (~line 1063) which removes the entire global hooks directory when `not project_path`.

Uninstall test files: All uninstall tests live in tests/cli/test_cli_install.py under TestUninstallCommand (12 tests). There are no per-installer test files like tests/cli/installers/test_windsurf.py.

In the gobby project, a pre-tool-use hook at ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py blocks Write operations unless a task has been created first. To create a task, use the mcp__gobby_tasks MCP server's create_task tool. The workflow is: check server status -> list tools -> call create_task with required params -> then Write is allowed.

This causes Claude Code pre-tool-use hook errors during development, breaking file edits until the hook is restored.

gobby-orchestration MCP server removed in commit d3e281be (task #8882). Its 11 tools across 5 modules (orchestrate, wait, monitor, review, cleanup) are replaced by the pipeline system. Key capability preserved: worktree merge-back-to-base now lives as merge_worktree in gobby-worktrees (backed by WorktreeGitManager.merge_branch). merge_clone_to_target was renamed to merge_clone. gobby-clones gained 7 new parity tools: claim_clone, release_clone, get_clone_by_task, link_task_to_clone, get_clone_stats, detect_stale_clones, cleanup_stale_clones. Storage layer got count_by_status, find_stale, cleanup_stale.

GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR env var controls where install_global_hooks() and uninstall write hook files. Default: ~/.gobby/hooks/. Set by conftest's protect_production_resources fixture to a temp dir for test safety. Always use os.environ.get('GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR', str(Path.home() / '.gobby' / 'hooks')) when referencing the global hooks directory. Without this, tests calling install_global_hooks() or uninstall would write/delete real production hooks at ~/.gobby/hooks/.

To create a task, use the mcp__gobby_tasks MCP server's create_task tool. The workflow is: check server status -> list tools -> call create_task with required params -> then Write is allowed.

In the gobby project, pre-tool-use hooks (hook_dispatcher.py) block Write operations unless a task has been created first via the mcp__gobby_tasks MCP server's create_task tool. The error message starts with 'You must...' and requires discovering available MCP tools, getting the create_task schema, and creating a task before file writes are permitted.

Bug pattern: uninstall tests deleting real user files. Root cause: uninstall_windsurf() in windsurf.py hardcoded `hooks_dir = Path.home() / '.gobby' / 'hooks'` instead of respecting GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR env var (which test fixtures set to isolate from real home). Additionally, --all uninstall tests only mocked uninstall_claude and uninstall_gemini, leaving windsurf/cursor/copilot/codex unmocked. When a developer has Windsurf installed, the real uninstall_windsurf ran and deleted ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py. Fix: (1) Use os.environ.get('GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR', ...) in windsurf.py:199, (2) Mock all 6 uninstallers in --all tests. Lesson: any test using --all flag must mock ALL uninstallers since auto-detect checks global paths and any CLI could be installed on any developer machine.

All installer uninstall functions must respect GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR env var for test isolation. Pattern: `Path(os.environ.get('GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR', str(Path.home() / '.gobby' / 'hooks')))`. Tests using --all flag must mock ALL uninstallers (claude, gemini, codex, copilot, cursor, windsurf), not just the ones being asserted on, because --all auto-detects installed CLIs on the developer's machine and will call any unmocked uninstaller for real. Fixed in windsurf.py:199 and test_cli_install.py (commit 8ae3fee7).

In the gobby project, Windsurf's global hooks/config are stored at ~/.codeium/windsurf/hooks.json. The uninstall_windsurf() function in windsurf.py (line 179+) accepts a mode parameter ('project' or 'global') that controls whether it targets project-level or global-level (~/.codeium/windsurf/) artifacts.

On macOS, chmod 444 (read-only) does NOT prevent file deletion — Unix file deletion is governed by the parent directory's write permission, not the file's. To make a file truly undeletable on macOS, use `chflags uchg <file>` which sets the immutable flag. Undo with `chflags nouchg <file>`. Useful for protecting critical files during debugging.

Bug: install.py uninstall function at line 1065 used Path.home() / '.gobby' / 'hooks' to delete global hooks, ignoring the GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR env var. This meant any test calling the uninstall CLI command would delete real ~/.gobby/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py. Fix: use os.environ.get('GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR', str(Path.home() / '.gobby' / 'hooks')). The install path (shared.py:install_global_hooks) already used GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR correctly — only the uninstall path was broken.

config.yaml is no longer a runtime config source (commit 45e22408). Runtime config now comes from: (1) bootstrap.yaml for 5 pre-DB settings (database_path, daemon_port, bind_host, websocket_port, ui_port), (2) DB config_store + Pydantic defaults for everything else. config.yaml is retained only for import/export via export_config_to_yaml(). The bootstrap module is at src/gobby/config/bootstrap.py. Hook dispatchers read bootstrap.yaml from ~/.gobby/bootstrap.yaml. CLI commands needing full config without a running daemon use load_full_config_from_db() from src/gobby/cli/utils.py. Agent-facing strings now point to gobby-config MCP tools instead of config.yaml.

When refactoring gobby's hook/dispatcher files, always ensure dev-mode symlinks in .claude/hooks/ are updated BEFORE deleting old source files. The safe order is: (1) create new target files, (2) update symlinks to point to new targets, (3) verify hooks work, (4) then delete old files. Reversing steps 3-4 causes unrecoverable session breakage.

Claude Code stop hooks that fail will retry repeatedly in a loop, flooding the user with error messages. Unlike pre-tool-use hooks which just block tool calls, broken stop hooks create an infinite cycle that cannot be escaped from within the session. The user must fix the underlying issue from a separate terminal.

In gobby, Claude Code hooks use a symlinked hook_dispatcher.py at .claude/hooks/hook_dispatcher.py (invoked via 'uv run python' with '--type=pre-tool-use|post-tool-use|stop' flags). If the symlink target is deleted (e.g., by deleting old per-CLI dispatcher files in src/gobby/install/), ALL hooks break simultaneously—pre-tool-use blocks all tool calls, and the stop hook enters an infinite error retry loop, making the session unrecoverable without manual terminal intervention.

Context window usage calculation: The context window (e.g. 200k for Claude) is an INPUT limit. Output tokens do NOT occupy it. Correct formula: context_load = input_tokens + cache_creation_input_tokens + cache_read_input_tokens. Percentage = context_load / context_window. Output tokens should be shown separately in tooltips but never added to the numerator. The DB field usage_input_tokens stores the total (uncached + cache components), so when hydrating uncachedInputTokens for display, compute: total - cache_read - cache_creation.

gobby installer modules (codex.py, copilot.py, claude) share common test patterns in tests/cli/test_installers_shared.py. When modifying installer interfaces (e.g., removing _is_dev_mode), both the individual installer tests AND the shared test file must be updated together.

In gobby, tests/conftest.py sets GOBBY_HOOKS_DIR as an environment variable fixture to prevent installer tests from writing to real hook directories. When adding new installer modules (like codex.py, copilot.py under src/gobby/cli/installers/), both the installer and its tests must account for this env var protection.
The gobby project has no existing installs/users other than the developer (josh), so breaking changes do not require migration infrastructure or backward compatibility support.

Gobby's hook dispatcher (in the dispatch script) already has two per-project opt-out mechanisms: it walks up from cwd to find .gobby/project.json for project detection, and supports flags like suppress_logs and has_source_detection. No new opt-out capability needs to be built for the per-project hook removal plan.

CLI commands init, install, and uninstall now support -C/--path to specify target directory. Before this, running from source required: `cd target && uv run --project  gobby init`. Now: `uv run --project  gobby init -C ~/Projects/target`. The option uses click.Path(exists=True, file_okay=False, path_type=Path) and resolves to replace Path.cwd(). initialize_project() already accepted a cwd parameter so no downstream changes were needed.

Gobby's hook dispatcher (hook_dispatcher.py) already has per-project opt-out mechanisms built in. The find_project_config() function (around line 462) walks up from cwd to find .gobby/project.json. Projects can disable hooks via this config. Feature flags include suppress_logs and has_source_detection (lines 80-82).
gobby has no existing installs/users other than Josh himself, so backward compatibility, migration infrastructure, and legacy support are unnecessary when making breaking changes.

The option uses click.Path(exists=True, file_okay=False, path_type=Path) and resolves to replace Path.cwd(). initialize_project() already accepted a cwd parameter so no downstream changes were needed.

Pre-commit mypy: Do NOT use mirrors-mypy repo — it runs mypy in an isolated venv missing most project deps, causing false positives. Use a local hook with `uv run mypy src/` instead, which gives mypy the full project environment and module graph. Fixed in commit 4418554d. The `pass_filenames: false` + `files: ^src/.*\.py$` combo means: only trigger the hook when src/ Python files change, but run mypy on the whole package (not individual files) for correct cross-module inference.

1. Frontend: web/src/hooks/useSettings.ts (DEFAULT_SETTINGS.chatMode)
2. Backend dataclass: src/gobby/servers/chat_session.py (chat_mode field default)
